source_paper_title: Theoretical Smoothing Frameworks for Nonsmooth Simple Bilevel Problems
context:
Section 5 (Concluding Remarks and Future Directions), page 30 (PDF pagination: 30/32)

Another important direction is to extend our smoothing frameworks to settings where the lower-level constraint set is not fixed but instead depends on the upper-level variable \(x\). A preliminary approach based on quadratic regularization combined with penalization has been proposed in [Liu et al., 2023]; however, it remains an open question whether the resulting approximation qualifies as a smoothing family in the sense of Definition 2.5 and whether it satisfies gradient consistency.
title: Gradient-consistent smoothing of the lower-level value function when the lower-level feasible set depends on the upper-level variable
latex:
Given integers \(n,m\ge 1\), let \(X\subseteq \mathbb{R}^n\) be closed and convex. For each \(x\in X\), let the lower-level feasible set be
\[
Y(x):=\{y\in \mathbb{R}^m: c(x,y)\le 0\},
\]
where \(c:X\times \mathbb{R}^m\to \mathbb{R}^p\) is a given (not necessarily smooth) constraint mapping and the inequality is interpreted componentwise. Let \(g:X\times \mathbb{R}^m\to \mathbb{R}\) be Lipschitz continuous (possibly nonsmooth). Define the lower-level value function
\[
v(x):=\inf_{y\in Y(x)} g(x,y)\in \mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}.
\]
A family \(\{v_\mu: \mu>0\}\) is called a smoothing family for \(v\) over an open set \(O\supseteq X\) if each \(v_\mu\) is continuously differentiable on \(O\) and
\[
\lim_{x\to \bar x,\,\mu\downarrow 0} v_\mu(x)=v(\bar x)\quad\text{for all }\bar x\in O.
\]
It is gradient-consistent at \(\bar x\in O\) if
\[
\varnothing\ne \limsup_{x\to \bar x,\,\mu\downarrow 0} \nabla v_\mu(x)\ \subseteq\ \partial v(\bar x),
\]
where \(\partial v(\bar x)\) denotes the Clarke subdifferential of \(v\) at \(\bar x\).

Problem: identify verifiable assumptions on \(g\), \(c\), and (possibly) on a concrete construction of \(v_\mu\) based on quadratic regularization and/or penalization of the constraints defining \(Y(x)\), such that \(\{v_\mu\}\) is a smoothing family for \(v\) (in the above sense) and satisfies gradient consistency at every \(\bar x\in O\) for which \(v(\bar x)\) is finite.
